courseBackground.title: 📖 Course background
courseBackground.author: Author
courseBackground.schoolValue: National Taipei University of Nursing and Health Sciences

description.title: 📖 About the project
description.body: NekoServe is a desktop application that simulates the customer service flow of a cat café. Using discrete-event simulation, it faithfully reproduces the full journey of each customer (arrival, queueing, seating, ordering, dining, interacting with cats, and leaving) and computes key service metrics. It is designed for classroom demonstration and service-system analysis. Version 1.0.0 adds batch replication with 95% confidence intervals, parameter sensitivity analysis, and classroom challenges, moving the teaching experience from single-run observation to statistical inference.

vision.title: 🚀 Project vision
vision.body: NekoServe started as a cat-café simulator for my System Simulation course, but underneath it is a general service-flow simulation engine. I've reframed it as a service-flow simulation platform, with the cat café as the first and currently only fully built service domain.
vision.roadmap: The service domain section on the Settings page shows the scenarios I'm planning to add: restaurant, clinic, bank, and salon. They're previews for now, but the architecture is ready to wire them onto the engine one at a time. This generalization direction is also the foundation I want to extend into a thesis topic.

architecture.title: 🏗️ System architecture
architecture.simulationLogic: Simulation logic lives entirely in Python + SimPy and is not duplicated in the UI. The Electron main process spawns the packaged Python executable and exchanges parameters and results as JSON over stdin/stdout.
architecture.ipc: IPC design follows Electron's security best practices: the renderer process can only talk to the main process through a whitelisted contextBridge API, with no direct access to Node.js or privileged Electron APIs.

distributions.title: 🎲 Random distributions
distributions.service: Order / prep / dining / interaction / rest times: normal distribution, std = mean × 0.2, floor at 1 minute
distributions.catRest: Cat rest trigger: after each interaction, sampled with probability catRestProbability

usage.title: 📌 How to use
usage.step1: Tweak parameters on the Settings page or pick a preset scenario
usage.step2: Click "Run simulation" and wait for it to finish (typically under 5 seconds)
usage.step3: Inspect KPI cards and charts on the Results page
usage.step4: Inspect the full event timeline on the Event Log page, filtering by type
usage.step5: Adjust parameters and re-run to compare scenarios side-by-side
usage.step6: Enable Batch mode to run multiple seeds and get averaged results with 95% confidence intervals
usage.step7: Enable Sensitivity analysis to sweep a single parameter and see how metrics respond
usage.step8: Click the Challenges button in the nav bar to try 8 guided exercises that test your understanding

experiment.title: 🔬 Designing simulation experiments
experiment.principle1Body: Change exactly one parameter at a time while keeping everything else (including the random seed) constant. For example, to study "how adding seats affects wait time," only adjust the seat count. This is the only way to attribute a change in results to your change in input.
experiment.principle2Title: Principle 2: fix the random seed for reproducibility
experiment.principle2Body: An identical seed generates an identical random stream, so two runs differ only in the parameter you tweaked. To study the effect of randomness itself, run multiple seeds and average the results for a robust estimate.
experiment.principle3BodyA: After several runs, switch to the Compare tab on the Results page to see KPIs for the last three runs side-by-side. Orange cells highlight spreads larger than 10%.
experiment.principle3BodyB: Metric priority: start with abandon rate (is the system overloaded?) → then utilization (which resource is the bottleneck?) → finally average wait time (customer experience).
experiment.principle4Title: Principle 4: deciding when results are trustworthy
experiment.principle4Item1: The simulation duration should be long enough (at least 20× the average stay time) so initial-transient effects wash out.
experiment.principle4Item2: Run 3–5 different seeds: if the spread across runs is within 5%, a single run is already stable enough.
experiment.principle4Item3: Sanity-check with Little's Law: N ≈ λ × W. Large deviations usually mean the run was too short.
experiment.principle5BodyA: Find the resource with the highest utilization (seats / staff / cats) and expand its capacity first. By the Theory of Constraints, investing in non-bottleneck resources barely moves system throughput.
experiment.principle5BodyB: Example: seats 95% utilized, staff 60% utilized. Adding staff will not shorten the seat queue; add seats first, or shorten dining time to turn tables faster.
experiment.principle6Body: A single simulation run is like a single dice roll: the result may be higher or lower than average. Enable Batch mode to run 10+ replications with different random seeds. The app computes the mean and 95% confidence interval automatically. A narrow interval means your estimate is precise; a wide interval means you need more replications. This is not optional overhead; it is standard DES methodology.

rpa.title: 🤖 RPA scenario sweep (course add-on)
rpa.description: An external Python + pyautogui bot drives NekoServe's UI as if it were a human, running 12 scenarios across catCount × staffCount and generating a comparison report. NekoServe itself is unchanged; this button is just an entry point.
rpa.warning1: The bot will take over your mouse and keyboard for 5 to 10 minutes
rpa.warning2: Do not touch the mouse or keyboard during the run; to abort, slam the mouse into the top-left corner
rpa.warning3: An HTML report opens automatically in your browser when the sweep finishes
rpa.statusRunningBot: RPA bot running (do not touch mouse / keyboard)
rpa.captureTitle: 🖼 First time? Capture the 8 UI templates
rpa.captureBody: pyautogui needs 8 small PNGs of NekoServe's buttons before it can find them. The button below walks you through 8 captures using the macOS crosshair, with a dialog telling you which tab / scroll position is needed each time. No terminal required.
rpa.smartCaptureHint: Smart mode lets NekoServe read its own DOM, navigate between pages, and capture each button automatically. Takes about 10 seconds. Templates 6 and 7 need a finished simulation first.
rpa.hintTemplatesMissing: 💡 The 7 UI template PNGs haven't been captured yet. Click the blue "Capture templates (7)" button above first; once calibration passes you can start the RPA sweep.
rpa.confirmModalBody: The bot will take over your mouse and keyboard for 5 to 10 minutes, running 12 scenarios. Do not touch mouse or keyboard during the run; to abort, slam the mouse into the top-left corner. Ready to start?
